title: Generic parameters not constrained to reference types should not be compared to null
description: Generic parameters not constrained to reference types should not be compared to "null"Analyze your codeintentionality-completereliabilityBugQuick FixIDE quick fixes available with SonarQube for IDEWhy is this an issue?How can I fix it?More InfoIn C#, without constraints on a generic type parameter, bothreferenceandvaluetypes can be passed. However, comparing this type parameter tonullcan be misleading as value types, likestruct, can never be null.Available In:Catch issues on the fly,in your IDEDetect issues in your GitHub, Azure DevOps Services, Bitbucket Cloud, GitLab repositoriesAnalyze code in youron-premise CI©2025SonarSource Sàrl. All rights reserved.Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Terms of Use
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant: if (param == null) // Noncompliant: T might be a value type
compliant: public void Method<T>(T param) where T : class
if (param == null) // Compliant: T is constrained to reference types

logic.detection_conditions: Detects null comparisons on unconstrained generic type parameters
logic.triggering_conditions: Triggers when: generic type parameter is compared to null without 'where T : class' constraint
logic.exclusion_conditions: Excludes: generic parameters with 'where T : class' constraint, EqualityComparer usage, comments
never_trigger_on: Generic parameters with 'where T : class' constraint
logic.reasoning: This rule detects comparisons of unconstrained generic type parameters to null. Without 'where T : class', T could be a value type, making null comparison misleading. A full implementation requires semantic analysis to: 1) Identify generic type parameters, 2) Check for class constraints, 3) Find null comparisons involving those parameters. This simplified version detects basic patterns like 'param == null' or 'param != null' in generic methods, though it cannot fully verify constraints without semantic context. This serves as a heuristic check.

should_trigger: public void Method<T>(T param) { if (param == null) { } }
should_not_trigger: public void Method<T>(T param) where T : class { if (param == null) { } }
